description:
You are an edge-computing engineer preparing a scraping utility that will run unattended on dozens of Linux-based IoT gateways.  
A miniature version of the workload has been staged for you under:

    /home/user/iot_dashboard/pages/

Inside that directory are several small, static HTML status pages—one per device.  
Your goal is to build a **headless, command-line scraping pipeline** that

1. Reads every HTML file that matches the glob:  
       /home/user/iot_dashboard/pages/device_*.html  
   It must work no matter how many files match that pattern.

2. Extracts from each page all of the following fields (every page contains exactly one occurrence of each):  
       Device ID    →  (HTML element with id="device-id")  
       Temperature  →  (HTML element with id="temp")  
       Humidity     →  (HTML element with id="hum")  
       Battery (%)  →  (HTML element with id="bat")  
       Timestamp    →  (HTML element with id="ts")  

3. Runs the scraping **concurrently**.  
   Hint: tools such as GNU parallel, xargs -P, or background subshells are acceptable; pick your favourite.

4. Writes two artefacts into a new directory named  
       /home/user/scrape_output/  
   which the script must create if it does not already exist:

   a) CSV summary: /home/user/scrape_output/device_metrics.csv  
      • Must be UTF-8 text.  
      • The very first line is an exact header:  
            device_id,temperature,humidity,battery,timestamp  
      • One data row per HTML page, **sorted by numerical device_id in ascending order**.  
      • Numeric fields (temperature, humidity, battery) must appear exactly as they were found—no extra units, no rounding.  
      • Example layout (values will differ):  
            device_id,temperature,humidity,battery,timestamp  
            001,23.1,51,88,2024-03-11T15:04:22Z  
            002,21.9,49,92,2024-03-11T15:04:34Z  

   b) Scrape log: /home/user/scrape_output/scrape.log  
      • One line per processed file in the exact format  
            Processed <filename>: <device_id>  
        where <filename> is the HTML file’s **basename** (e.g. device_001.html).  
      • The lines may appear in any order (because the work is parallel), but there must be exactly one line for each HTML file that was present before the script ran.

5. Ensure the exit status of your scraping command(s) is zero when successful.

6. The automated grader will confirm:
      • /home/user/scrape_output/ exists.  
      • device_metrics.csv follows the header and row format listed above, contains every device once, and is sorted numerically.  
      • scrape.log contains the correct “Processed …” lines.  
      • No intermediate temporary files remain outside /home/user/scrape_output/.

Remember: you do not have root access.  All work must be achievable with user-level permissions and standard Debian/Ubuntu CLI tools available inside the container environment.

The grader will not inspect how you scraped—only that the two output files are exactly correct and were produced via commands that could reasonably execute in parallel.  Good luck!
truth: Initial fixture files (already present before the agent starts): Directory: /home/user/iot_dashboard/pages/ 1. /home/user/iot_dashboard/pages/device_001.html Contents (1-line, no surrounding spaces): 00123.750872024-03-11T15:04:22Z 2. /home/user/iot_dashboard/pages/device_002.html 00221.949922024-03-11T15:04:34Z 3. /home/user/iot_dashboard/pages/device_003.html 00324.548902024-03-11T15:04:47Z Expected artefacts after successful run: A) /home/user/scrape_output/device_metrics.csv Exact UTF-8 content including newline characters (\n) after every line: device_id,temperature,humidity,battery,timestamp\n 001,23.7,50,87,2024-03-11T15:04:22Z\n 002,21.9,49,92,2024-03-11T15:04:34Z\n 003,24.5,48,90,2024-03-11T15:04:47Z\n B) /home/user/scrape_output/scrape.log Each line terminated by \n. The three lines may be in any order, but each must appear exactly once: Processed device_001.html: 001\n Processed device_002.html: 002\n Processed device_003.html: 003\n
